perturbation.original_text: 8.4 CHOICE OF LAW; FORUM SELECTION. This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of California without reference to its choice of law rules. Sponsor hereby irrevocably consents to personal jurisdiction and venue in the state and federal courts located in San Francisco, California with respect to any actions, claims or proceedings arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.4 CHOICE OF LAW; FORUM SELECTION. This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of a jurisdiction of Snap's choosing. Sponsor consents to jurisdiction and venue in a forum of Snap's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the specification of California law and San Francisco as the jurisdiction for disputes. By allowing Snap to choose the jurisdiction, it enables the possibility of selecting a jurisdiction that offers fewer consumer protections, potentially violating California consumer protection laws if those laws would otherwise apply based on the consumer's location or the nature of the transaction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1785.20 states that consumer credit reporting agencies must follow specific procedures for reinvestigating disputed information. By allowing Snap to choose a different jurisdiction, this clause could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent these consumer protections if the consumer is a California resident, thus violating California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.6. CONSUMER CREDIT REPORTING AGENCIES ACT [1785.1 - 1785.36] ( Title 1.6 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 1271. ) CHAPTER 3. Requirements on Users of Consumer Credit Reports [1785.20 - 1785.22] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 1271. ) 1785.20. (a) If any person takes any adverse action with respect to any consumer, and the adverse action is based, in whole or in part, on any information contained in a consumer credit report, that person shall do all of the following: (1) Provide written notice of the adverse action to the consumer. (2) Provide the consumer with the name, address, and telephone number of the consumer credit reporting agency which furnished the report to the person. (3) Provide a statement that the credit grantor’s decision to take adverse action was based in whole or in part upon information contained in a consumer credit report. (4) Provide the consumer with a written notice of the following rights of the consumer: (A) The right of the consumer to obtain within 60 days a free copy of the consumer’s consumer cr
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the specific jurisdiction of California and allows Snap to choose any jurisdiction. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1785.20, as it could circumvent consumer protections for California residents if Snap chooses a jurisdiction with weaker laws, directly undermining the intent of California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates the obligations of persons taking adverse action against consumers, which could be undermined.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, Snap will not grant any third party any right to sponsor any products or services in the Exclusive Category on or through the Snap Web Site.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, Snap may grant third parties rights to sponsor products or services, with such arrangements subject to Snap's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the exclusivity provision, which could potentially allow Snap to partner with competitors of Sponsor in ways that harm Sponsor's brand or market position. In California, the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to a contract to not do anything that would injure the right of the other party to receive the benefits of the agreement. By removing the exclusivity clause, Snap could act in ways that undermine the benefits Sponsor expects to receive from the agreement, potentially violating this implied covenant.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California's Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification violates California Commercial Code § 1201 which imposes a duty of good faith. This duty requires honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing. By removing the exclusivity clause, Snap opens the door to act opportunistically to harm the benefit for United Airlines.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessi
perturbation.justification: The original contract prohibited Snap from granting third parties rights to sponsor products or services in the Exclusive Category. The changed text allows Snap to grant such rights, effectively removing the exclusivity provision. The Law Explanation argues this violates the duty of good faith under California Commercial Code § 1201. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines various terms within the Commercial Code but does not explicitly define 'good faith' or directly address exclusivity clauses. However, the Perturbation Explanation states that this change could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California law, as removing the exclusivity clause allows Snap to undermine the benefits the Sponsor expects to receive from the agreement. Thus, the removal of the exclusivity clause contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, supporting the existence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall take, at a minimum, measures consistent with those taken to protect its own similar types of Confidential Information (and in any event, at least reasonable measures) to protect the other's Confidential Information against disclosures prohibited by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall take measures they deem appropriate to protect the other's Confidential Information against disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the confidentiality protections by removing the 'at a minimum' clause and the requirement to take 'at least reasonable measures.' This introduces ambiguity and allows a party to potentially take inadequate steps to protect confidential information. California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By allowing for potentially unreasonable measures, the modified text contradicts CUTSA if the confidential information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: This is a violation of Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1, because this law protects trade secrets when reasonable measures have been taken to protect the secret. By weakening the confidentiality requirement the parties take the risk that protections may not meet this minimum threshold and thus they are exposed if they deem any confidential information to be a trade secret.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Der
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens confidentiality protections by removing the 'at a minimum' and 'at least reasonable measures' clauses. This contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1, as explained in the Law Explanation, because that law protects trade secrets when reasonable measures are taken. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' which suggests a standard of care. By allowing potentially unreasonable measures, the modified text creates a risk that the protections may not meet the minimum threshold for trade secret protection under California law. The omission of the 'reasonable measures' standard directly undermines the expected level of protection for confidential information, leading to a clear contradiction.
